1.synthesis: Paul opens with the certainty of eschatological hope: if the earthly tent of the body is destroyed, believers have a building from God — a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens (2 Corinthians 5:1). In this tent they groan, longing to be clothed over with the heavenly dwelling, not to be found unclothed but further clothed, so that what is mortal may be swallowed by life (2 Corinthians 5:2–4). God has prepared them for this, having given the Spirit as a down payment (2 Corinthians 5:5). Therefore they walk by faith, not by sight; they are of good courage and prefer to be away from the body and present with the Lord (2 Corinthians 5:6–8). The governing ambition is to be pleasing to him, for all must appear before the judgment seat of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:9–10).
1.historical_context: The tent metaphor (skēnos) for the body was used in Greek philosophy — Plato's Phaedo described the body as an anchor holding the soul earthward — but Paul transforms the image: his concern is not the soul's escape from matter but the resurrection body replacing the mortal one. The verb ependysasthai ('clothe over,' v.4) implies putting on over an existing garment — transformation rather than mere survival. The bema (judgment seat, v.10) was the elevated platform in Roman city centers where magistrates rendered binding verdicts; the Corinthians knew it well — Gallio had sat on Corinth's bema when Paul was dragged before him (Acts 18:12–17).
1.literary_structure: The section moves from confident knowledge (v.1: 'we know...') through groaning (v.2) and fear of nakedness (v.3) to the Spirit as earnest guarantee (v.5). The resolution of groaning is not escape but transformation: 'swallowed by life' reverses the death-swallows language (1 Corinthians 15:54). Verses 6–8 apply the hope practically: walk by faith, not sight. Verses 9–10 close with the spur of judgment: the ambition to be pleasing to the Lord is grounded in eschatological accountability, not moral self-improvement.
1.theological_application: Chrysostom reads the eschatological vision as motivational rather than escapist: Paul 'arouses their zeal because many trials drew on' — the certainty of the heavenly dwelling is precisely what keeps believers from being 'confounded' by present affliction. The groaning is not self-pity but the appropriate response of those who know they were made for more than this. Gill on v.10 notes that appearing before the judgment seat is universal and necessary — 'to give up our account, for we are here on trial' (Barnes) — but for those in Christ this is not judgment unto condemnation but stewardship assessment. The terror of the Lord that motivates Paul (v.11) is not the slavish fear of one who does not know if he is accepted, but the reverent accountability of one who already stands in grace and knows it. The Spirit as earnest (v.5) grounds present assurance: God himself has prepared believers for the transformation ahead.
1.christology: The heavenly dwelling is 'from God' — resurrection life is not a human achievement but a divine gift, 'eternal in the heavens.' The Spirit given as earnest is the Spirit of Christ, the first installment that guarantees the full inheritance and ensures that 'absent from the body' means 'present with the Lord' (v.8). The judgment seat is 'of Christ' — the risen and exalted Lord is the eschatological judge before whom the whole created order must give account. He who died for all so that those who live should live no longer for themselves but for him (v.15) is the same one who will render each person according to what was done — not as adversary but as sovereign Lord whose verdict is the most important fact of the believer's existence.

11.synthesis: Knowing the fear of the Lord, Paul persuades people; his life is open before God and he hopes before their consciences (2 Corinthians 5:11). The love of Christ constrains him: if one died for all, then all died; he died that those who live should live not for themselves but for him (2 Corinthians 5:14–15). Henceforth Paul regards no one according to the flesh. If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation: the old has passed away; the new has come (2 Corinthians 5:16–17). All this is from God, who through Christ reconciled Paul to himself and gave him the ministry of reconciliation — God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them (2 Corinthians 5:18–19). Therefore believers are ambassadors for Christ; they implore on Christ's behalf: be reconciled to God. For God made him who knew no sin to be sin, so that in him they might become the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:20–21).
11.historical_context: The phrase kainē ktisis ('new creation,' v.17) carries the weight of Jewish apocalyptic expectation: 1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, and Isaiah anticipated a cosmic new creation at the end of the age. Paul announces that this new creation has already arrived in the person of those who are in Christ — the age to come has broken into the present. The great exchange of v.21 — 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' — reflects both the Levitical ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin offering, Leviticus 4–5), in which the sacrifice becomes identified with the sin it bears, and the scapegoat rite of Leviticus 16:21–22.
11.literary_structure: The passage pivots on the death of Christ as the hinge of all re-evaluation. The logic runs: Christ's death is for all → all died → those who live live for him → this generates a new mode of knowing (v.16) → which is grounded in new-creation status (v.17) → which flows from the reconciling act of God (v.18). The passage then turns from indicative to imperative: the reconciliation accomplished is the content of the apostolic message ('be reconciled to God'), and the great exchange of v.21 is both the theological grounding and the rhetorical climax.
11.theological_application: Gill reads 'new creation' as both a secret reality from eternity (the elect considered in Christ) and an open reality at conversion: 'faith does not put a man into Christ, but makes him appear to be in him.' The new creation is not self-improvement or moral renewal but ontological change — 'the old has passed away; behold, the new has come.' On v.19, Gill notes that 'God was in Christ reconciling' means God 'drew the scheme, fixing the method of reconciliation; his thoughts were employed about it, which were thoughts of peace; he called a council of peace' — reconciliation is a divine initiative from beginning to end. On v.20, Gill distinguishes the ambassador's role precisely: 'come not to propose terms of peace, to treat with men about it, to offer it to them, but to publish and proclaim it as made by him.' The church's proclamation is not negotiating reconciliation but announcing it as already accomplished.
11.christology: The great exchange of v.21 is the christological center of the entire letter: 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' Gill notes the layers: Christ appeared 'in the likeness of sinful flesh,' was traduced as a sinner, and was made 'a sin offering' — bearing the sin he never committed, becoming in himself the antitype of all the Levitical sacrifices. But more than this, he was 'made sin itself' so that the righteousness achieved through him could be reckoned to those who are in him. The 'love of Christ' that constrains (v.14) is the same love that moved him to the cross: those who receive the ministry of reconciliation have themselves been reconciled by this same exchange, and their ambassadorial service is the overflow of what they have first received.
